name: Saints & Church Fathers
description: Wisdom on faith, love, and the soul from the great saints and fathers of the Church.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: You have made us for yourself, O Lord, and our heart is restless until it rests in you.
quotes.source: Confessions, Book 1, Chapter 1 (c. 400)
quotes.notes: The opening prayer of the Confessions; Henry Chadwick translation of the Latin 'fecisti nos ad te et inquietum est cor nostrum.'

quotes.content: Late have I loved you, beauty so old and so new: late have I loved you.
quotes.source: Confessions, Book 10, Chapter 27 (c. 400)
quotes.notes: Augustine's lament over his slowness to turn to God; Henry Chadwick translation of 'Sero te amavi, pulchritudo tam antiqua et tam nova.'

quotes.content: Grant me chastity and continence, but not yet.
quotes.source: Confessions, Book 8, Chapter 7 (c. 400)
quotes.notes: Augustine recalls his younger self's half-hearted prayer; translation of 'da mihi castitatem et continentiam, sed noli modo.'

quotes.content: Love, and do what you will.
quotes.source: Homilies on the First Epistle of John, Homily 7, Section 8 (c. 407)
quotes.notes: From Augustine's tractate on 1 John; translation of 'Dilige, et quod vis fac,' meaning right love roots right action.

quotes.content: Two cities have been formed by two loves: the earthly by the love of self, even to the contempt of God; the heavenly by the love of God, even to the contempt of self.
quotes.source: The City of God, Book 14, Chapter 28 (c. 420)
quotes.notes: Augustine's defining contrast of the earthly and heavenly cities; Marcus Dods translation.

quotes.content: Faith is to believe what you do not see; the reward of this faith is to see what you believe.
quotes.source: Sermon 43, Section 1 (c. 400)
quotes.notes: From Augustine's sermon on Isaiah 7:9; standard English rendering of 'Fides est credere quod non vides.'

quotes.content: Grace does not destroy nature but perfects it.
quotes.source: Summa Theologica, Part I, Question 1, Article 8 (c. 1270)
quotes.notes: From Aquinas's reply on sacred doctrine; translation of 'gratia non tollit naturam, sed perficit,' rendered by the Dominican Fathers edition.

quotes.content: Man proposes, but God disposes.
quotes.source: The Imitation of Christ, Book 1, Chapter 19 (c. 1420)
quotes.notes: Traditional English translation of 'Homo proponit, sed Deus disponit'; a Kempis popularized the maxim through the Imitation.

quotes.content: At the Day of Judgment we shall not be asked what we have read, but what we have done.
quotes.source: The Imitation of Christ, Book 1, Chapter 3 (c. 1420)
quotes.notes: From the chapter on the knowledge of truth; traditional English translation.

quotes.content: Love feels no burden, thinks nothing of trouble, attempts what is above its strength.
quotes.source: The Imitation of Christ, Book 3, Chapter 5 (c. 1420)
quotes.notes: From the chapter on the wonderful effect of divine love; traditional English translation.

quotes.content: Vanity of vanities, all is vanity, except to love God and to serve Him alone.
quotes.source: The Imitation of Christ, Book 1, Chapter 1 (c. 1420)
quotes.notes: From the opening chapter on imitating Christ; traditional English translation echoing Ecclesiastes.

quotes.content: Let nothing disturb you, let nothing frighten you. All things are passing; God never changes. God alone suffices.
quotes.source: Poesias ('Nada te turbe'), bookmark verses (c. 1580)
quotes.notes: The 'Nada te turbe' verses found in Teresa's breviary after her death; standard English translation of the Spanish original.

quotes.content: The soul of the just is nothing else but a paradise, in which God takes His delight.
quotes.source: The Interior Castle, First Mansions, Chapter 1 (1577)
quotes.notes: Opening image of the Interior Castle; E. Allison Peers translation of the Spanish 'Las Moradas.'

quotes.content: In the evening of life, we will be judged on love alone.
quotes.source: Sayings of Light and Love, No. 59 (c. 1585)
quotes.notes: From the 'Dichos de Luz y Amor'; Kavanaugh and Rodriguez translation of 'a la tarde te examinaran en el amor.'

quotes.content: If you are what you should be, you will set the whole world ablaze.
quotes.source: Letter to Stefano Maconi (c. 1376)
quotes.notes: From Catherine's letters urging fervor of love; the popular English rendering of her exhortation to become fully oneself in God.

quotes.content: Take, Lord, and receive all my liberty, my memory, my understanding, and my entire will, all that I have and possess.
quotes.source: The Spiritual Exercises, Contemplation to Attain Love, No. 234 (c. 1548)
quotes.notes: The 'Suscipe' prayer that closes the Spiritual Exercises; Louis Puhl translation of the Latin original.

quotes.content: All shall be well, and all shall be well, and all manner of thing shall be well.
quotes.source: Revelations of Divine Love, Chapter 27 (c. 1395)
quotes.notes: The words Julian receives from Christ in her showings; Grace Warrack's Middle English edition.

quotes.content: He said not: Thou shalt not be tempested, thou shalt not be travailed, thou shalt not be afflicted; but He said: Thou shalt not be overcome.
quotes.source: Revelations of Divine Love, Chapter 68 (c. 1395)
quotes.notes: Julian's consolation from her sixteenth showing; Grace Warrack's Middle English edition.

quotes.content: He became man that we might become divine.
quotes.source: On the Incarnation, Section 54 (c. 318)
quotes.notes: Athanasius's classic statement of deification; translation of 'autos gar enenthropesen, hina hemeis theopoiethomen.'

quotes.content: The bread in your cupboard belongs to the hungry; the coat unused in your closet belongs to the one who needs it.
quotes.source: Homily 'I Will Tear Down My Barns' (c. 368)
quotes.notes: From Basil's homily on Luke 12:18 against hoarding wealth; standard English translation of the Greek homily.

quotes.content: Do you wish to honor the body of Christ? Do not ignore him when he is naked. Do not honor him here in the church with silk, only to neglect him outside where he is cold and naked.
quotes.source: Homilies on the Gospel of Matthew, Homily 50 (c. 390)
quotes.notes: Chrysostom links the Eucharist to care for the poor; translation from the Nicene and Post-Nicene Fathers edition.

quotes.content: Not all of us can do great things. But we can do small things with great love.
quotes.source: A Simple Path (1995)
quotes.notes: A documented saying of Mother Teresa published in the collected spiritual counsels 'A Simple Path.'

quotes.content: The reason for loving God is God himself; the measure, to love him without measure.
quotes.source: On Loving God, Chapter 1 (c. 1128)
quotes.notes: From Bernard's treatise 'De Diligendo Deo'; translation of 'Causa diligendi Deum, Deus est; modus, sine modo diligere.'

quotes.content: The glory of God is a living man, and the life of man consists in beholding God.
quotes.source: Against Heresies, Book 4, Chapter 20, Section 7 (c. 180)
quotes.notes: The often-shortened 'glory of God is man fully alive' comes from this passage; Ante-Nicene Fathers translation.

quotes.content: The blood of the martyrs is the seed of the Church.
quotes.source: Apologeticus, Chapter 50 (c. 197)
quotes.notes: Standard English rendering of 'semen est sanguis Christianorum,' Tertullian's reply to Roman persecution.

quotes.content: He can no longer have God for his Father who has not the Church for his mother.
quotes.source: On the Unity of the Catholic Church, Chapter 6 (c. 251)
quotes.notes: Cyprian's statement on ecclesial unity; Ante-Nicene Fathers translation of 'De Unitate Ecclesiae.'

quotes.content: Ignorance of the Scriptures is ignorance of Christ.
quotes.source: Commentary on Isaiah, Prologue (c. 408)
quotes.notes: From the prologue to Jerome's commentary on Isaiah; translation of 'Ignoratio scripturarum, ignoratio Christi est.'

quotes.content: I saw the snares that the enemy spreads out over the world and I said groaning, 'What can get through from such snares?' Then I heard a voice saying to me, 'Humility.'
quotes.source: The Sayings of the Desert Fathers, Anthony, Saying 7 (c. 350)
quotes.notes: From the 'Apophthegmata Patrum'; Benedicta Ward translation of the Greek alphabetical collection.

quotes.content: Idleness is the enemy of the soul.
quotes.source: The Rule of St. Benedict, Chapter 48 (c. 530)
quotes.notes: From the chapter on daily manual labor; translation of 'Otiositas inimica est animae.'

quotes.content: Listen carefully, my child, and attend with the ear of your heart.
quotes.source: The Rule of St. Benedict, Prologue (c. 530)
quotes.notes: The opening words of the Rule; translation of 'Ausculta, o fili, et inclina aurem cordis tui.'

quotes.content: I will spend my heaven doing good on earth.
quotes.source: Story of a Soul, recorded last conversations (1897)
quotes.notes: A deathbed saying recorded in her final conversations and published with 'Story of a Soul'; standard English translation.
